## Columbia Law School Announces Four Major Faculty Appointments and Two Endowed Chair Designations

### Incoming Faculty Strengthen the Law School's Programs in Antitrust, Constitutional Law, International Tax, and Critical Race Theory

### Incoming Faculty Strengthen the Law School's Programs in Antitrust, Constitutional Law, International Tax, and Critical Race Theory

**NEW YORK** — Dean Adelaide Vasquez-Brindisi announced today four new permanent faculty appointments at Columbia Law School, effective with the 2023–2024 academic year, together with the designation of two existing faculty members to named professorial chairs. The new appointments — which follow more than eighteen months of extended faculty searches — represent the largest single cohort of tenured and tenure-track hires at the Law School since the 2014–2015 academic year, and reflect what the Dean described in remarks to the faculty earlier this week as "a deliberate, generational investment in the intellectual depth of the school across multiple subject areas of strategic importance."

In addition, two existing members of the Columbia Law faculty have been designated to named chairs:

- **Professor Caleb Wenzhou Halberstam** has been designated the Michael I. Sovern Professor of Law, succeeding the late Professor Lance Liebman.
- **Professor Marisela Quiñones-Eastman** has been designated the Isidor and Seville Sulzbacher Professor of Law.

---

### About the New Appointments

#### Professor Bartolomeo Calligari-Ng — Henry W. and Joan T. Voorhees Professor of Law

---

### About the New Appointments

#### Professor Bartolomeo Calligari-Ng — Henry W. and Joan T. Voorhees Professor of Law

Professor Calligari-Ng, who joins Columbia Law School from Berkeley Law, is one of the country's most cited antitrust scholars under the age of fifty. His scholarship draws on industrial-organization economics, regulatory theory, and competition-law doctrine, and is concerned principally with the analysis of vertical integration in digital platforms and with the institutional design of antitrust enforcement.

His major works include *Vertical Lines: Doctrine and Discipline in Modern Antitrust* (Harvard University Press, 2019); *The Platform Conglomerate* (Yale Law Journal, vol. 130, no. 4, 2021); and the casebook *Antitrust Law and Economics* (with Drosselmeier and Yashin, West Academic, 5th ed. 2022).

Professor Calligari-Ng received his JD from Yale Law School in 2007 and his PhD in Economics from Princeton University in 2012. Before joining Berkeley in 2014, he served as a senior economist in the Antitrust Division of the U.S. Department of Justice and clerked for Judge Stephen Reinhardt on the Ninth Circuit Court of Appeals.

"Bart Calligari-Ng is the rare antitrust scholar whose work is read with equal seriousness by economists, doctrinal scholars, and enforcement officials," said Professor Reinaldo Quintanilla-Stoyanov, chair of the antitrust search committee. "His decision to join us represents a significant strengthening of our program in regulation and political economy."

The Voorhees Chair was established in 1987 by the late Henry W. Voorhees, LL.B. 1952, and his wife Joan, and has previously been held by Professors Edmund S. Ferentino (1987–2003) and Lavonne Pidgeon-Mathers (2004–2022).

---

#### Professor Saoirse Murchadh-Renwick — Professor of Law

---

#### Professor Saoirse Murchadh-Renwick — Professor of Law

Professor Murchadh-Renwick, who joins Columbia Law School from the University of Chicago Law School, is a constitutional law scholar whose work bridges originalism and democratic theory. Her current book project, *The Senate and the Constitution: A Structural History*, is under contract with Princeton University Press.

She received her JD from Stanford Law School in 2010 and an MPhil in Politics from the University of Oxford (Balliol College) in 2007. She clerked for Judge Diane S. Sykes on the Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals and for Justice Sonia Sotomayor on the United States Supreme Court.

Her articles include "The Senate as a Federalism Institution" (*Yale Law Journal*, vol. 132, no. 6, 2023, with Hortense Bellweather-Onuchukwu); "Originalism and the Lower-Court Judge" (*Harvard Law Review*, vol. 134, no. 8, 2021); and "What the Reconstruction Senate Knew" (*University of Chicago Law Review*, vol. 88, no. 3, 2021).

"Professor Murchadh-Renwick is the kind of constitutional law scholar who is read across the methodological divide," said Professor Wakefield Underbrink-Yoshioka, chair of the constitutional law search committee. "Her work will substantially strengthen our public-law offerings, and our students will benefit enormously from her arrival in the classroom."

---

#### Professor Hiroshi Akinwumi-Delacroix — Associate Professor of Law

---

#### Professor Hiroshi Akinwumi-Delacroix — Associate Professor of Law

Professor Akinwumi-Delacroix, who joins Columbia Law School from NYU School of Law, is an international tax scholar whose work focuses on the institutional design of multilateral tax instruments, on the OECD BEPS framework, and on the political economy of tax cooperation among developing economies.

He received his JD from Harvard Law School in 2013 and his LL.M. in Taxation from NYU in 2014, and is a graduate (with first-class honours) of the University of Lagos. Before entering academia, he practiced international tax at Skadden, Arps, Slate, Meagher & Flom LLP in New York and at Mori Hamada & Matsumoto in Tokyo.

His recent publications include "The Two-Pillar Solution: A Critical Reconstruction" (*Tax Law Review*, vol. 76, no. 2, 2023); "Tax Cooperation and the Global South" (*Columbia Journal of Transnational Law*, vol. 60, no. 4, 2022); and "Treaty Shopping Revisited" (*Florida Tax Review*, vol. 25, no. 1, 2021).

---

#### Professor Ndidi Rosalind Babatunde-Cariou — Patricia Williams Professor of Law

---

#### Professor Ndidi Rosalind Babatunde-Cariou — Patricia Williams Professor of Law

Professor Babatunde-Cariou, who joins Columbia Law School from Georgetown Law as the inaugural Patricia Williams Professor of Law, is a scholar of critical race theory, civil procedure, and the history of American legal thought. Her award-winning book *The Color of Procedure: Race and Civil Justice in the Twentieth Century* (Harvard University Press, 2022) received the 2023 Order of the Coif Book Award and the 2023 American Society for Legal History Cromwell Book Prize.

She received her JD from Yale Law School in 2008 and her PhD in History from Princeton University in 2014. She clerked for Judge Damon Keith on the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals and served as Special Assistant to the Assistant Attorney General for Civil Rights, U.S. Department of Justice, from 2009 to 2010.

"It is a particular honor to welcome Professor Babatunde-Cariou as the inaugural holder of the Patricia Williams Chair," said Dean Vasquez-Brindisi. "Her scholarship continues, deepens, and complicates the intellectual project to which Professor Williams gave her career."

The Patricia Williams Professorship was established in 2022 through a $7.5 million gift from Reuben and Sigrid Habermehl-Brzezinski (LL.B. 1968) honoring the late Professor Patricia J. Williams, who taught at Columbia Law School from 1991 to 2020 and was the James L. Dohr Professor of Law at the time of her retirement.

---

### Designation of Two Existing Faculty to Named Chairs

#### Professor Caleb Wenzhou Halberstam — Michael I. Sovern Professor of Law

Professor Halberstam, who joined the Columbia Law faculty in 2014, has been designated the Michael I. Sovern Professor of Law, succeeding the late Professor Lance Liebman, who held the chair from 1991 until his death in 2020 and after whom Vermont Law School has named its visiting-scholars program in employment law.

Professor Halberstam is a scholar of administrative law and federal courts. His book *The Administrative State's Constitution* (Yale University Press, 2021) was awarded the 2022 American Bar Association Silver Gavel Award and the 2022 Robert L. Stern Award. He received his JD from Yale Law School in 2003 and clerked for Judge Stephen Breyer on the First Circuit and for Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg on the United States Supreme Court.

The Michael I. Sovern Chair was established in 2002 by friends and colleagues of the late President Emeritus Michael I. Sovern, LL.B. 1955.

#### Professor Marisela Quiñones-Eastman — Isidor and Seville Sulzbacher Professor of Law

#### Professor Marisela Quiñones-Eastman — Isidor and Seville Sulzbacher Professor of Law

Professor Quiñones-Eastman, who joined the Columbia Law faculty in 2008, has been designated the Isidor and Seville Sulzbacher Professor of Law. A scholar of immigration law, constitutional law, and human rights, she received her JD from Yale Law School in 1998 and her DPhil in Politics from the University of Oxford (Nuffield College) in 2002.

Her recent works include *The Borders of Citizenship* (Harvard University Press, 2020); "Plyler at Forty" (*Columbia Law Review*, vol. 122, no. 5, 2022); and the casebook *Immigration and Citizenship: Process and Policy* (with Aleinikoff, Martin, and Motomura, West Academic, 9th ed. 2021).

The Isidor and Seville Sulzbacher Chair was established in 1986 by the family of Isidor Sulzbacher, LL.B. 1929, and has previously been held by Professors Henri DeWitt-Vasilescu (1986–2004) and Margery Ashmolean-Quinn (2005–2022).

---

### Dean's Statement

"These appointments and designations reflect a deliberate strategy that the faculty and I have pursued over the last several years," Dean Vasquez-Brindisi said. "We are committed to a Columbia Law School whose intellectual breadth genuinely reflects the breadth of the modern legal profession, and we are committed to making the resources of the school available to the next generation of scholars who will define that profession."

"It is also a particular pleasure," the Dean continued, "to see two of our most accomplished mid-career colleagues recognized with chairs that connect them, intellectually and institutionally, to the long traditions of this school."

---

### About Columbia Law School

Founded in 1858, Columbia Law School educates lawyers, scholars, and leaders for service to the bar, to the academy, and to society. Among the school's distinguished alumni are President Franklin D. Roosevelt, Justice Ruth Bader Ginsburg, and Attorney General Eric Holder. The school enrolls approximately 1,250 JD students, 300 LL.M. students, and 25 JSD students. It has a full-time faculty of 130.

For more information about Columbia Law School, please visit law.columbia.edu.

### Backgrounder: The Search Process

The four faculty appointments announced today are the result of search processes that, in each case, were initiated by faculty resolution between September 2021 and April 2022 and were chaired in each case by a senior tenured member of the Columbia Law faculty. The composition of each search committee, and the search committee's report to the faculty, are matters of internal faculty governance. The following committee chairs may, however, be identified:

- **Antitrust and Industrial Organization search:** Chaired by Professor Reinaldo Quintanilla-Stoyanov.
- **Constitutional Law search:** Chaired by Professor Wakefield Underbrink-Yoshioka.
- **International Tax search:** Chaired by Professor Margery Halberstrom-Cavendish.
- **Critical Race Theory and Civil Procedure search:** Chaired by Professor Kwabena Dansoman-Ferreira.

The two endowed chair designations, by contrast, do not result from formal faculty searches and are made by the Dean in consultation with the relevant standing faculty committees. The designations announced today were approved by the Faculty Appointments Committee at its meeting of February 24, 2023.

### Backgrounder: The Patricia Williams Chair

### Backgrounder: The Patricia Williams Chair

The Patricia Williams Professorship of Law was established at Columbia Law School in 2022 through a $7.5 million gift from the alumni couple Reuben Habermehl-Brzezinski (Columbia Law LL.B. 1968) and Sigrid Habermehl-Brzezinski. The gift, announced at the Law School's June 2022 reunion, is the largest single endowed-chair gift in the Law School's history.

The chair honors the late Professor Patricia J. Williams, who served on the Columbia Law faculty from 1991 to 2020 and held the James L. Dohr Professorship from 2010 until her retirement. Professor Williams is the author of, among other works, *The Alchemy of Race and Rights* (Harvard University Press, 1991), *The Rooster's Egg: On the Persistence of Prejudice* (Harvard University Press, 1995), and *Seeing a Color-Blind Future: The Paradox of Race* (Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 1997). She was a MacArthur Fellow (2000) and a recipient of the American Bar Association's Margaret Brent Women Lawyers of Achievement Award (2008).

The Habermehl-Brzezinski gift establishes, in addition to the chair, an annual public lecture in Professor Williams's honor and a graduate fellowship program supporting JSD students working in the broad area of race and the law.

### Backgrounder: The Michael I. Sovern Chair

The Michael I. Sovern Professorship of Law was established at Columbia Law School in 2002 through a fund-raising campaign organized by alumni of Columbia Law School graduating between 1955 and 1985, in honor of Michael I. Sovern (1931–2020), who served as President of Columbia University from 1980 to 1993 and as Dean of Columbia Law School from 1970 to 1979.

President Sovern was, before his administrative service, a scholar of labor law, administrative law, and dispute resolution. He held the Chancellor Kent Professorship and, after his return to the faculty in 1993, the Sovern Professorship (the latter having been redesignated in his honor at the conclusion of his presidency). The chair created in 2002 — the Michael I. Sovern Professorship of Law — is distinct from the latter designation and was held from its establishment in 2002 until 2020 by the late Professor Lance Liebman.

Professor Liebman, the former Dean of Columbia Law School (1991–1996) and Director of the American Law Institute (1999–2014), held the Sovern Chair from 2002 until his death on November 4, 2020. The designation of Professor Halberstam to the chair, announced today, is the first designation since Professor Liebman's death.

### Backgrounder: The Isidor and Seville Sulzbacher Chair

The Isidor and Seville Sulzbacher Professorship of Law was established at Columbia Law School in 1986 by the family of Isidor Sulzbacher, LL.B. 1929, and his wife Seville, in recognition of Isidor Sulzbacher's distinguished career at the New York commercial law firm of Sulzbacher, Goldsmith & Marcus (now part of a successor firm) and of the family's longstanding support of the Law School.

The chair has previously been held by Professors Henri DeWitt-Vasilescu (1986–2004) and Margery Ashmolean-Quinn (2005–2022). Professor Ashmolean-Quinn, the former Vice Dean for Curriculum, retired from the Columbia Law faculty in May 2022.

### Backgrounder: The Voorhees Chair

The Henry W. and Joan T. Voorhees Professorship of Law was established at Columbia Law School in 1987 through a $4.2 million gift from Mr. Henry W. Voorhees, LL.B. 1952, and his wife Joan. Mr. Voorhees, who died in 2009, was a senior partner at the New York law firm of Voorhees, Lemkin & Mansfield and served as President of the New York City Bar Association from 1987 to 1989. The chair has previously been held by Professors Edmund S. Ferentino (1987–2003) and Lavonne Pidgeon-Mathers (2004–2022).

### Backgrounder: 2023–2024 Faculty Composition

With the appointments announced today, the Columbia Law School faculty for the 2023–2024 academic year will comprise 130 full-time members, of whom:

- 87 are tenured Professors of Law
- 14 are tenure-track Associate Professors of Law
- 11 are Clinical Professors of Law
- 8 are Lecturers in Law
- 10 are Professors of Practice

Female faculty constitute 47.7% of the full-time faculty, an increase of approximately three percentage points over five years ago. Faculty of color constitute 39.2% of the full-time faculty, an increase of approximately seven percentage points over five years ago.

The full faculty roster is published annually in the Law School's *Bulletin of Information* and is available on the Law School's website.

The full faculty roster is published annually in the Law School's *Bulletin of Information* and is available on the Law School's website.

### A Note on the Dean's Faculty Hiring Initiative

The appointments announced today are the final cohort to be hired under the Dean's Faculty Hiring Initiative, an institutional program launched at the start of Dean Vasquez-Brindisi's deanship in July 2020 with the stated objective of recruiting a total of fifteen new tenured or tenure-track faculty members over a three-year period. With today's announcement, the Initiative has met its numerical goal.

The Dean's Office is in the early stages of planning a successor initiative for the deanship's second term, the principal contours of which will be presented to the faculty at the September 2023 faculty retreat.

### Backgrounder: A Note on the Dean's Curricular Initiatives

The faculty appointments announced today are intended to support several of the Dean's curricular initiatives, including:

The faculty appointments announced today are intended to support several of the Dean's curricular initiatives, including:

- **The Initiative on Race, Law, and Society** — established 2021. The Initiative supports the development of an integrated curriculum in race and the law spanning the first-year Civil Procedure course, an upper-level Critical Race Theory survey, and a series of advanced seminars and workshops. Professor Babatunde-Cariou will serve as Faculty Director of the Initiative.
- **The Initiative on Regulation and Political Economy** — established 2020. The Initiative supports interdisciplinary teaching and research at the intersection of administrative law, antitrust, financial regulation, and political economy. Professor Calligari-Ng will join the Faculty Steering Committee of the Initiative.
- **The Initiative on Constitutional Democracy** — established 2022. The Initiative supports curricular and scholarly engagement with questions of constitutional design, democratic institutions, and the rule of law. Professor Murchadh-Renwick will serve as Co-Director of the Initiative alongside Professor Underbrink-Yoshioka.
- **The Initiative on International Tax** — established 2019. The Initiative supports the Law School's leading position in international tax scholarship and teaching. Professor Akinwumi-Delacroix will join the Faculty Steering Committee.

### A Concluding Note from the Dean

"The Columbia Law School we are building is a school for the next generation of legal practice and legal scholarship — a school that takes seriously both the deep traditions of doctrinal rigor that have always defined this institution and the urgent, contemporary questions that the law is now asked to address," Dean Vasquez-Brindisi said in remarks to the faculty earlier this week.

"The colleagues we are welcoming today, and the colleagues we are recognizing today with these chair designations, represent that synthesis. They are scholars of the first rank, teachers of demonstrated excellence, and intellectual leaders whose work will define the conversations in their fields for the next several decades. The faculty's enthusiastic embrace of their candidacies is, I think, the clearest possible signal that the school's intellectual culture is in robust health."

The Dean's full remarks to the faculty are available on the Law School's internal faculty website. The Dean will deliver a public lecture on the state of the Law School at the September 2023 Convocation; the lecture will be open to alumni and members of the public and will be live-streamed via the Law School's website.

# Global Arbitration Review

## Vance to Chair Brazil–Europe Gas Infrastructure Arbitration

*ICC tribunal to hear $2 billion-plus dispute over offshore capacity allocation*

**By Imogen Hartwell-Beauclerc** | Reporter, GAR | 6 March 2024

---

The Columbia Law professor-emerita and full-time arbitrator **Eleanor Vance** has been confirmed as chair of a three-member ICC tribunal that will hear a multi-billion-dollar dispute between a Brazilian state-controlled energy company and a consortium of European pipeline operators over offshore gas-infrastructure capacity, GAR has learned.

Two persons with direct knowledge of the constitution of the tribunal said the appointment had been made in late February and formally confirmed by the ICC Court last week. The proceeding, which is seated in Paris and governed by Swiss substantive law as a matter of the underlying contract, arises out of a 2014 joint operating agreement covering offshore gas infrastructure off the south-eastern Brazilian coast. The amount in dispute is understood to exceed US $2 billion.

The claimants, identified by the same sources as the European consortium members, allege that capacity-allocation decisions taken by the Brazilian operator between 2020 and 2022 contravened the preferential-access provisions of the JOA. The respondent denies the allegations and is expected to advance a number of counterclaims of its own.

### Counsel

Counsel for the claimant consortium are understood to be a team from **Three Crowns LLP**, while the respondent is understood to be represented by **Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer**. Neither firm responded to GAR's request for comment, citing the confidentiality of the proceeding.

### The Chair

### The Chair

Professor Vance, 59, is among a relatively small group of senior U.S. legal academics to have made the full transition from academic life to a substantial independent arbitration practice. She took emerita status at Columbia Law School in 2022 having joined that school's faculty in 2001 (after a five-year period at Georgetown Law), and she was appointed to the Charles Evans Hughes Professorship of Law at Columbia in 2009. She practises from a chambers on Park Avenue in Midtown Manhattan.

A Rhodes Scholar who took her first degree in law at Oxford in 1988, Professor Vance went on to earn her JD from Yale in 1991. She thereafter clerked for the late Judge Patricia Wald of the United States Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit before entering the academy. Her co-authored treatise *The Law and Practice of Commercial Arbitration* — written with Professor Robert Kaminski of NYU Law — is in its third edition and is widely assigned in both American and English LL.M. arbitration courses.

Speaking to GAR, Professor Kaminski described his co-author and longtime collaborator as "exceptionally well suited" to a complex, high-value commercial chair. "Eleanor combines doctrinal seriousness with procedural discipline in a way that is genuinely rare," he said. "She is willing to write long, fully reasoned awards — which is increasingly what parties want when they know that an annulment challenge is likely to follow."

### Recent Appointments

Professor Vance has chaired or sat as sole arbitrator in upwards of eighty commercial matters over the last decade. Among her recent publicly reported chair appointments are:

- A US $1.4 billion ICC-administered post-acquisition indemnification dispute, in which she issued a partial final award on liability against the seller-respondents in 2020;
- An LCIA-administered reinsurance dispute seated in London, in which she sat as chair.

She has also been active in institutional governance. She served on the **ICC Commission on Arbitration and ADR** between 2015 and 2021 and presently sits on the board of the **New York International Arbitration Center**.

### The Procedural Calendar

### The Procedural Calendar

Persons close to the proceeding told GAR that the tribunal is expected to convene a first procedural conference later in the spring, with an evidentiary hearing now provisionally scheduled for early 2025. A final award is unlikely to issue before the end of 2025 at the very earliest, and persons familiar with similar matters in the ICC's energy-sector caseload suggested that an award before mid-2026 would be regarded as comparatively quick.

### Context: Brazilian Offshore Gas Disputes

### Context: Brazilian Offshore Gas Disputes

The new proceeding adds to a growing portfolio of high-value commercial arbitrations arising from the post-2010 expansion of Brazilian offshore gas infrastructure. Practitioners contacted by GAR observed that capacity-allocation provisions in pre-2015 JOAs covering Brazilian offshore assets were drafted at a time when the market structure and the regulatory environment looked quite different from how they look now, and that disputes of the type now before Professor Vance's tribunal were broadly foreseeable.

"You had a generation of contracts written for a world that was about to change very substantially," one London-based partner told GAR. "It is genuinely unsurprising that we are now seeing the disputes that those contracts were always going to produce."

### Why It Matters

The matter is the latest in a series of high-value commercial arbitrations in which a U.S. academic-turned-arbitrator has been appointed as chair of an ICC tribunal seated outside the United States. Some practitioners regard this as reflective of a broader internationalisation of the chair-arbitrator market, in which institutional appointments are increasingly being made on the basis of subject-matter expertise and procedural reputation rather than national affiliation.

"Five or ten years ago, you might have expected this kind of chair appointment to go to a continental European arbitrator with an energy-sector background," one Paris-based partner told GAR. "What you are seeing now is a market in which the best chairs are appointed on substantive grounds, and Eleanor Vance is a very strong substantive choice for this kind of matter."

Professor Vance was reached by email but, as is her usual practice in respect of pending matters, declined to comment.

The ICC International Court of Arbitration did not respond to a request for comment, in line with its practice of declining to confirm or deny the existence or particulars of pending proceedings.

### Sidebar: Vance's Recent ICC Practice

Professor Vance's recent reported chair and co-arbitrator appointments at the ICC include the following matters, each of which has been publicly reported in at least one of the major arbitration trade publications:

- A 2020 partial final award in a $1.4 billion ICC-administered post-acquisition indemnification dispute, in which Professor Vance — sitting as chair — issued a partial final award on liability against the seller-respondents. The award is understood by GAR's sources to have been the subject of a subsequent annulment challenge, which was unsuccessful.
- An LCIA-administered reinsurance dispute seated in London, in which Professor Vance sat as chair. The proceeding was settled in 2022 following a partial final award on a coverage issue.
- A 2019 ICC-administered shareholder dispute concerning a Spanish renewables-developer joint venture, in which Professor Vance sat as a party-appointed co-arbitrator.
- A 2018 ICC-administered commodity-supply dispute concerning a long-term LNG supply agreement, in which Professor Vance sat as chair.

GAR understands that Professor Vance is also currently sitting as co-arbitrator in a confidential SCC matter and as chair of a confidential UNCITRAL ad hoc proceeding administered by the PCA. These matters have not been publicly reported and are mentioned here only at a high level of generality.

### Sidebar: The Three Crowns / Freshfields Match-Up

### Sidebar: The Three Crowns / Freshfields Match-Up

The two principal counsel teams in the new ICC proceeding — Three Crowns LLP for the European consortium and Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer for the Brazilian state-owned operator — represent a familiar pairing in major ICC energy-sector matters. The two firms have appeared opposite one another in at least seven publicly reported major ICC energy matters since 2018, with broadly comparable outcomes across the series. The new proceeding adds a further significant matter to that series and is expected to feature, on Three Crowns's side, a team led by partners Camille Forrester-DuBois (Paris) and Hendrik van Doorslaer-Reinhart (London), and on Freshfields's side, a team led by partner Constance Bellechasse-Maillard (Paris) and counsel Edouard Vasseur-Beaumont (London). None of the firms has confirmed the composition of the respective teams, however, and GAR cautions readers that the composition may shift before the proceeding's first procedural conference.

### Sidebar: Annulment Risk and the Reasoned Award

A theme that recurs in practitioner discussion of major ICC energy-sector chair appointments is the question of annulment risk, and in particular the question of whether the chair is prepared to write a long, fully reasoned award that will survive a Paris-seat annulment challenge under the French Code of Civil Procedure or, where relevant, an enforcement challenge under the New York Convention.

Professor Vance's reputation in this respect is that of an arbitrator who is willing to write — and indeed insists on writing — substantially longer and more fully reasoned awards than the institutional median. Her best-known award, the 2020 partial final award in the post-acquisition indemnification matter referred to above, is understood to have run to 412 pages in length, including detailed treatments of each of the principal contractual interpretation issues, of the parties' factual and expert evidence, and of the governing law. GAR's sources for the present brief told us that this approach is one of the qualities that the parties' counsel were understood to value when they were in a position to confirm or to oppose her appointment as chair.

It is worth noting in this connection that the underlying contract in the present proceeding — the 2014 joint operating agreement — is governed by Swiss substantive law as a matter of contract, while the seat of arbitration is Paris. Annulment of any final award would therefore be sought, if at all, in the Paris Court of Appeal under French annulment law; enforcement of the award (likely in Brazil, in France, and in one or more European jurisdictions where the consortium members are domiciled) would be governed by the New York Convention. The interaction between the Swiss substantive law applicable to the merits and the French procedural law applicable to the annulment proceeding is a matter to which both Professor Vance and her co-arbitrators (when their identities are confirmed) will need to attend with some care.
### Sidebar: Professor Vance's Park Avenue Chambers

### Sidebar: Professor Vance's Park Avenue Chambers

Professor Vance practises from chambers at a Park Avenue address in Midtown Manhattan. The chambers, which she opened in 2022 following her transition to emerita status at Columbia Law School, are understood to comprise three full-time professional staff — a chief of chambers (Ms Pilar Echevarría-Hutchings), a junior research associate, and an administrative assistant — and to accept appointments principally in commercial, energy, M&A, and reinsurance disputes. She is also available to sit in ICSID and ICSID Additional Facility matters.

### Sidebar: The Wider Context of Brazilian State-Owned Energy Disputes

---

### Sidebar: The Wider Context of Brazilian State-Owned Energy Disputes

Brazilian state-owned energy companies have, since approximately 2018, become substantially more active in international commercial arbitration than they were in the preceding decade. The reasons for this shift are multiple and include: (a) the maturation of a generation of long-term contracts entered into during the 2010s commodity boom; (b) the substantial regulatory reforms to the Brazilian oil-and-gas sector that followed the conclusion of the so-called Lava Jato investigations; (c) the recalibration of capacity-allocation and offtake arrangements following the entry of new private-sector participants into the Brazilian upstream and midstream markets; and (d) the broader global shift in energy markets following the 2022 Russian invasion of Ukraine and the associated disruption to European gas supply.

The combined effect of these developments has been to put substantial pressure on the commercial arrangements that govern the relationship between Brazilian state-owned operators and their European, North American, and Asian counterparties. Many of those arrangements — including the 2014 JOA that underlies the new proceeding — were entered into in a market environment that no longer exists, and disputes over the interpretation and application of those arrangements have, perhaps unsurprisingly, multiplied.

GAR's *Brazilian Arbitration Watch* tracker, which is maintained by the GAR research team and updated quarterly, identified twenty-three publicly reported commercial-arbitration proceedings involving Brazilian state-owned energy companies as of the end of 2023, compared with eleven as of the end of 2020 and four as of the end of 2017. The new proceeding involving Professor Vance is the largest of those twenty-three matters by reported amount in dispute.

### Sidebar: The Park Avenue Independent Arbitrator Market

### Sidebar: The Park Avenue Independent Arbitrator Market

The expansion of the U.S. independent-arbitrator market — and in particular the emergence of a substantial cohort of senior academic-turned-arbitrator practitioners practising from Manhattan and Washington chambers — has been one of the more interesting structural developments in the U.S. arbitration profession over the past decade.

The trend reflects, in part, the maturation of the U.S. arbitration bar, the growth of full-service institutional arbitration in New York (centered on the New York International Arbitration Center and the ICDR), and the recognition by parties and counsel that the chair appointment market is increasingly internationalised. It also reflects, more specifically, the emergence of a "second career" model for distinguished legal academics who have completed their primary academic careers and who wish to bring their substantive expertise to bear on commercial dispute resolution.

Professor Vance is among the most prominent of this cohort. Other senior U.S. academics with substantial full-time arbitration practices include — without limitation — Professor Robert Kaminski of NYU Law (Professor Vance's longtime treatise co-author), Professor Catherine M. Whitmer of Harvard Law (now of counsel at her own chambers in Cambridge), and Professor Theodore Bockstein of the University of Chicago (who maintains chambers on Wacker Drive). The cohort as a whole is characterised by a willingness to write substantially longer awards than the institutional median, a strong subject-matter focus, and a comparatively high acceptance rate of chair (as opposed to party-appointed co-arbitrator) appointments.

The structure of the Park Avenue chambers model — small staff, high billing rate, intensive matter-by-matter engagement — is comparatively unfamiliar to the European arbitration market, which is dominated by partners in major commercial law firms who maintain arbitration practices as one element of a broader transactional and dispute-resolution practice. The two models are, in many respects, in productive competition, and the new ICC proceeding chaired by Professor Vance — in which the parties are represented by major European-headquartered law firms but the chair sits independently from her own chambers — is a characteristic illustration of how the two models can interact in a single major matter.

### Sidebar: The 2014 JOA Generation of Disputes

### Sidebar: The 2014 JOA Generation of Disputes

The JOA that underlies the new ICC proceeding was signed in 2014, a year that marked, in retrospect, an inflection point in the global energy market. The substantial expansion of U.S. shale production over the preceding several years had begun to put downward pressure on global oil and gas prices, and 2014 closed with the first major commodity-price correction of the post-2008 cycle.

Many of the joint operating agreements signed in 2014 — including the agreement that underlies the new proceeding — were therefore drafted in a market environment that was about to undergo substantial change. The capacity-allocation provisions of those agreements, in particular, were drafted on assumptions about future production volumes and future capacity utilisation that the subsequent decade did not validate.

The result has been a substantial wave of disputes — now beginning to materialise as formal proceedings — concerning the interpretation and application of those provisions. Practitioners on both the claimant and respondent sides told GAR that the new ICC proceeding is likely to be one of perhaps five or six "generational" matters in this category over the next several years, each likely to involve amounts in dispute in the range of US $1 billion to US $3 billion and each likely to be of substantial precedential significance for the interpretation of similar provisions in the broader JOA universe.

# "The Tribunal's Job Is to Decide" — A Conversation with Prof. Eleanor Vance

*The following is an edited transcript of a conversation conducted in February 2024 for the* Global Arbitration Review *podcast,* The Hearing. *The interviewer is GAR senior reporter Lila Chen.*

---

**Chen:** Professor Vance, thank you for joining us. You've had an unusual career path — full-time academic for nearly two decades, and now one of the busier sole arbitrators on the New York scene. Why the pivot?

**Vance:** Thank you for having me. I'd push back gently on the word "pivot" — I started taking arbitral appointments while I was still teaching, probably around 2008 or so. By the mid-2010s I was sitting on maybe six or seven matters a year alongside teaching, and at some point the calendar just had to give. I took emerita status at Columbia in 2022 and now I'm full-time in chambers.

**Chen:** You write notoriously long awards. Is that the academic in you?

**Chen:** You write notoriously long awards. Is that the academic in you?

**Vance:** Partly. But it's also a deliberate choice. If a party is going to challenge an award at the seat — and in some of the jurisdictions I sit in, that's a near-certainty — the tribunal owes the parties and the supervisory court a reasoned record. I'd rather write 180 pages than have a sloppy 40-page award vacated on a *Mitsubishi* or manifest-disregard theory.

**Chen:** Speaking of which — your 2018 *Columbia Law Review* piece on manifest disregard. Has the doctrine moved at all since then?

**Chen:** Speaking of which — your 2018 *Columbia Law Review* piece on manifest disregard. Has the doctrine moved at all since then?

**Vance:** Not in any direction I find coherent. The Second and Ninth Circuits still treat it as alive in some attenuated form; the Fifth has flatly rejected it post-*Hall Street*. I think we're a decade away from the Supreme Court taking it up again, if ever.

**Chen:** Let's talk practice mechanics. You're known for being expensive. Is that fair?

**Chen:** Let's talk practice mechanics. You're known for being expensive. Is that fair?

**Vance:** *(laughs)* I charge what the market charges for a chair with my profile. My current rate is twelve hundred dollars an hour, with the usual ICC- and AAA-style caps when those apply. I'm told that's at the lower end for U.S.-based arbitrators with international caseloads. I don't take retainers, and I don't take cancellation fees for hearings that move by more than thirty days' notice.

**Chen:** Any recent matters you can talk about in general terms?

**Chen:** Any recent matters you can talk about in general terms?

**Vance:** Only what's already on the public docket. I chaired an ICC matter — Meridian Capital — that involved a post-acquisition indemnity dispute over an oil and gas SPA. I issued a partial award on liability in late 2021. The case is illustrative of a trend I've been seeing: private equity buyers using arbitration as a forum for what are essentially accounting disputes. I'm not sure that's the best use of the institution, but it's where the work is.

**Chen:** What do you say to younger lawyers who want to build an arbitrator practice?

**Chen:** What do you say to younger lawyers who want to build an arbitrator practice?

**Vance:** Read widely. Learn at least one other language well enough to read pleadings — I do my own reading in French and Italian when matters allow it, and it changes how you understand a record. And be patient. Most arbitrators don't get their first chair appointment until they're well into their forties.

**Chen:** Prof. Vance, thank you.

**Vance:** My pleasure.

# Law360 — Arbitration

## Vance Tapped To Chair $2B Cross-Border Energy Arbitration

**By Marcus Hellinger** | March 4, 2024, 6:42 PM EST

Prof. Eleanor Vance, the Columbia Law professor-turned-arbitrator known for her work on high-stakes commercial disputes, has been appointed chair of a three-member ICC tribunal hearing claims valued at more than $2 billion between a Brazilian state-owned energy company and a consortium of European pipeline operators, two sources with knowledge of the matter told Law360 on Monday.

The dispute, seated in Paris and governed by Swiss substantive law, arises from a 2014 joint operating agreement covering offshore gas infrastructure. The claimants allege that capacity-allocation decisions made between 2020 and 2022 breached preferential-access provisions in the JOA. Counsel for the parties — understood to include teams from Three Crowns and Freshfields — declined to comment, citing confidentiality.

Vance, 59, is among a small group of U.S. academics who have built substantial full-time arbitration practices since leaving the classroom. She joined the Columbia Law School faculty in 2001 after five years at Georgetown, and was appointed the Charles Evans Hughes Professor of Law in 2009. She took emerita status in 2022 to focus on her chambers practice, which she runs from offices on Park Avenue in Midtown Manhattan.

"Eleanor brings a rare combination of doctrinal depth and procedural discipline," said Robert Kaminski of NYU Law, her longtime co-author. "She is willing to write long, reasoned awards, which parties increasingly value when they know an annulment challenge is likely."

A Rhodes Scholar who took her first law degree at Oxford in 1988 before earning her J.D. from Yale in 1991, Vance clerked for Judge Patricia Wald on the D.C. Circuit before joining the academy. Her treatise with Kaminski, *The Law and Practice of Commercial Arbitration*, is in its third edition and is widely assigned in U.S. and U.K. LL.M. programs.

Vance has chaired or sat as sole arbitrator in more than 80 commercial matters over the past decade. Recent publicly reported appointments include a $1.4 billion post-acquisition indemnification dispute administered by the ICC in 2020 — in which she issued a partial final award on liability against the seller-respondents — and an LCIA reinsurance matter seated in London.

She is also active in institutional governance. Vance served on the ICC Commission on Arbitration and ADR from 2015 to 2021 and currently sits on the board of the New York International Arbitration Center.

Sources close to the new ICC proceeding said the tribunal is expected to hold a procedural conference later this spring, with an evidentiary hearing likely to take place in early 2025. A final award would not be expected before late 2025 at the earliest.

*— Editing by Sarah Donovan.*

# PARTIAL FINAL AWARD ON LIABILITY

**In the Matter of an Arbitration Under the Rules of the International Chamber of Commerce**

**ICC Case No. 25,118/MHM**

**MERIDIAN CAPITAL PARTNERS, L.P.**
(Claimant)

— v. —

**ARDEN HOLDINGS, INC.** and **ARDEN ENERGY SUBHOLDCO II, LLC**
(Respondents)

---

**Sole Arbitrator:** Prof. Eleanor M. Vance
**Seat of Arbitration:** New York, New York
**Date of Award:** 14 November 2021

---

## I. Introduction and Procedural Posture

---

## I. Introduction and Procedural Posture

1. This Partial Final Award addresses liability arising out of disputes under a Stock Purchase Agreement dated 3 March 2018 (the "SPA"), pursuant to which Claimant Meridian Capital Partners, L.P. ("Meridian") acquired one hundred percent (100%) of the issued and outstanding equity interests of Arden Energy Subholdco II, LLC from Respondents (collectively, "Arden") for a base purchase price of USD 612,000,000, subject to customary adjustments and an earn-out of up to USD 95,000,000 tied to 2019–2020 EBITDA milestones (the "Transaction").

2. By Request for Arbitration dated 22 January 2020, Meridian asserted claims for (i) breach of the representations and warranties set forth in Article IV of the SPA, in particular Sections 4.07 (Financial Statements), 4.11 (Absence of Undisclosed Liabilities), and 4.18 (Environmental Matters); (ii) fraudulent inducement; and (iii) indemnification under Article IX. Arden filed its Answer and Counterclaim on 6 March 2020, asserting that Meridian had improperly withheld earn-out payments due in respect of fiscal year 2019.

3. The Tribunal was constituted on 14 May 2020. The Terms of Reference were signed by the Parties and the Sole Arbitrator on 31 July 2020. A two-week evidentiary hearing was conducted in New York from 8 March 2021 through 19 March 2021, with closing submissions filed on 28 May 2021.

## II. Applicable Law

4. Section 10.07 of the SPA provides that the agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its conflict-of-laws principles. The Parties do not dispute the application of Delaware substantive law to the merits, nor the application of the Federal Arbitration Act, 9 U.S.C. § 1 *et seq.*, and Article 21 of the ICC Rules (2017) to procedural questions.

## III. Findings on the Section 4.07 Claim

## III. Findings on the Section 4.07 Claim

5. Meridian contends that the audited financial statements for fiscal year 2017, attached as Schedule 4.07(a) to the SPA, materially overstated EBITDA by approximately USD 23.4 million through the improper capitalization of recurring workover expenses at the Permian Basin assets. The Tribunal finds, on the basis of the expert report of Mr. Halvorsen and the contemporaneous email traffic admitted as Exhibits C-188 through C-212, that the capitalization practice was inconsistent with the Company's stated accounting policies and with GAAP as articulated in ASC 360-10-25.

6. The Delaware Supreme Court has held that a buyer asserting a breach of a financial-statement representation must establish (i) the existence of the representation, (ii) its falsity at the relevant date, and (iii) loss. *See Cobalt Operating, LLC v. James Crystal Enters., LLC*, 2007 WL 2142926, at \*27 (Del. Ch. July 20, 2007); *see also Akorn, Inc. v. Fresenius Kabi AG*, 2018 WL 4719347 (Del. Ch. Oct. 1, 2018). Each element is satisfied here.

7. The Tribunal accordingly finds Arden liable for breach of Section 4.07 of the SPA. Quantum shall be determined in a subsequent phase pursuant to Procedural Order No. 7.

## IV. Disposition

## IV. Disposition

## Argentine Mining Giant Taps Veteran Swiss Arbitrator To Chair $3.4B ICC Treaty Dispute

**By Sasha Wendelin** | June 28, 2023, 4:12 PM EDT

The ICC International Court of Arbitration has confirmed the constitution of a three-member tribunal to hear a $3.4 billion treaty-based dispute between Argentine state-owned mining company Yacimientos Mineros Federales SA ("YMF") and a Toronto-listed claimant, North Andes Lithium Corp., over the 2022 cancellation of two lithium-brine concessions in Salta Province, three sources with direct knowledge of the proceeding told Law360 on Wednesday.

The tribunal will be chaired by **Dr. Annika Beilenberg-Roth**, a Zurich-based independent arbitrator and former partner at Lalive, whose appointment was made by the ICC Court after the party-appointed co-arbitrators failed to agree on a chair candidate within the ninety-day window set by the procedural calendar. The party-appointed arbitrators are **Professor Hernán Saldaña-Cifuentes** (nominated by the claimant) of the Universidad Torcuato Di Tella in Buenos Aires, and **Dr. Patrick O'Halloran SC** (nominated by the respondent), a Dublin-based barrister and former Irish Attorney General.

### Background

### Background

The dispute arises from the November 2022 cancellation by the Salta provincial government, allegedly at the direction of the Argentine federal government, of two mining concessions held by North Andes Lithium Corp.'s wholly owned Argentine subsidiary, Salar Norte Litio SA. The concessions covered approximately 47,000 hectares in the Salar de Olaroz–Cauchari basin and were originally granted in 2017 under the Argentina–Canada bilateral investment treaty.

North Andes alleges that the cancellation was effected without due process, in breach of the BIT's fair-and-equitable-treatment standard, and in furtherance of an effective expropriation of approximately $2.8 billion in committed development capital. The company also alleges that the cancellation was preceded by a sustained pattern of harassment, including the cancellation of customs clearances, the obstruction of equipment imports, and the targeted application of provincial tax assessments later struck down by the Argentine federal courts.

The Argentine state denies the substantive allegations and raises a jurisdictional objection grounded in the BIT's denial-of-benefits clause, asserting that North Andes Lithium Corp. is a shell entity controlled by Chinese principals through a Toronto holding-company structure and is therefore not entitled to BIT protection.

### Counsel

The claimant is represented by a team from Three Crowns LLP led by partners **Camille Forrester-DuBois** and **Jeremiah Olabisi-Penrose**, with associate counsel **Sayed-Reza Mostafavi-Hardcastle**. The respondent is represented by Foley Hoag LLP, led by partners **Karoline Brüning-Whetstone** and **Mauricio Sandoval-Eriksson**, with counsel from the Argentine Procuración del Tesoro de la Nación.

Counsel for both parties declined to comment when contacted by Law360.

### The Tribunal

Dr. Beilenberg-Roth, who chairs the tribunal, is widely regarded as one of the most active continental arbitrators of her generation in Latin American investment-treaty matters. She sat as co-arbitrator in *Compañía Energética del Cono Sur v. Bolivia* (ICSID Case No. ARB/19/14, award rendered 2022) and is currently presiding over a $1.1 billion gas-transport dispute administered by the ICC and seated in Geneva. She was admitted in Zurich in 1995 and holds a doctorate in international law from the University of Geneva.

Professor Saldaña-Cifuentes, the claimant's nominee, is a professor of international economic law at the Universidad Torcuato Di Tella and a former Argentine Minister of Justice and Human Rights (briefly, in 2015). His scholarship is concerned principally with the jurisdictional architecture of investment treaty arbitration and with the relationship between international investment law and domestic public-law institutions. He has sat in approximately fifteen prior investment-treaty matters, most often as a party-appointed arbitrator for claimants.

Dr. O'Halloran SC, the respondent's nominee, was Attorney General of Ireland from 2014 to 2016 and has served as counsel and arbitrator in a range of commercial and public-international-law matters since returning to private practice. He is a member of Brick Court Chambers' international arbitration panel and is widely regarded as one of the leading public-international-law practitioners in the Irish bar.

According to two of the sources, the ICC Court considered a number of candidates for chair before settling on Dr. Beilenberg-Roth. Arbitrators understood to have been previously considered but not selected include Sir Quentin Marlborough-Stoddart KC of Essex Court Chambers, Maître Cécile Vannier-Lafont of Vannier Lafont Avocats in Paris, Prof. Eleanor Vance of Columbia Law School (now in independent practice), Justice Kgomotso Setlhare (Ret.) of the South African Supreme Court of Appeal, and Prof. Yoshiro Tatsumi-Aoki of Keio University.

### Seat, Procedural Calendar, and Substantive Law

### Seat, Procedural Calendar, and Substantive Law

The tribunal will sit in Paris, with the ICC's hearing facilities at the Court of Arbitration's headquarters at 33-43 Avenue du Président Wilson available for procedural conferences and an evidentiary hearing scheduled provisionally for the autumn of 2024. The substantive law of the dispute is principally the Argentina–Canada BIT, together with applicable rules of customary international law.

The procedural calendar set out in Procedural Order No. 1, issued on June 19, contemplates the following principal milestones:

The procedural calendar set out in Procedural Order No. 1, issued on June 19, contemplates the following principal milestones:

- Statement of Claim: October 31, 2023
- Statement of Defense (with any jurisdictional objections): March 28, 2024
- Reply on Merits / Counter-Memorial on Jurisdiction: July 30, 2024
- Rejoinder on Merits / Reply on Jurisdiction: October 15, 2024
- Evidentiary Hearing: November 18–29, 2024
- Post-Hearing Briefs: February 14, 2025

A final award is not expected before the third quarter of 2025.

### Argentina's Recent Investment Treaty Caseload

A final award is not expected before the third quarter of 2025.

### Argentina's Recent Investment Treaty Caseload

The new YMF / North Andes matter is the eleventh investment-treaty proceeding pending against the Republic of Argentina or against an Argentine sub-sovereign entity as of mid-2023, and the third arising from a mining-or-mineral-rights dispute in northern Argentina. It follows the December 2022 commencement of *Cariboo Northern Resources v. Argentina* (ICSID Case No. ARB/22/47, also concerning lithium-brine concessions) and the February 2023 commencement of *Andinor Copper SA v. Argentina* (ICSID Case No. ARB/23/4, concerning copper concessions in Catamarca Province).

The Procuración del Tesoro estimates the total amount in dispute across pending investment-treaty proceedings against Argentina at approximately $11.8 billion as of May 2023, against approximately $7.4 billion as of the same date in 2022. The substantial year-over-year increase is attributable principally to the lithium and copper claims.

### Funding

### Funding

North Andes Lithium Corp. confirmed in a February 2023 disclosure to Canadian securities regulators that the claimant's costs in connection with the proceeding are being financed under a non-recourse arbitration finance facility provided by a major third-party funder, the identity of which has not been publicly disclosed. The terms of the facility, including the funder's expected return on a successful outcome, are likewise undisclosed; market participants told Law360 that the facility is understood to be in the range of $14 million to $18 million.

### Outlook

### Outlook

Practitioners told Law360 that the proceeding is likely to be closely watched for several reasons. First, the jurisdictional objection — grounded in alleged Chinese beneficial ownership of a Canadian claimant — squarely presents a denial-of-benefits question that has been the subject of considerable doctrinal interest but limited reasoned authority. Second, the case is among the first major mining-sector investment-treaty proceedings to arise from the post-2020 expansion of lithium-brine concessioning across the Andean triangle (Argentina, Bolivia, Chile). And third, the substantial third-party funding involvement is likely to occasion procedural skirmishing over disclosure obligations under the ICC's 2021 rules on funder identification.

"This is a case that is going to make law in three or four different directions," one Buenos Aires-based observer told Law360. "There's a real possibility that the award, when it issues, will be cited in casebooks for a generation."

A spokesperson for North Andes Lithium Corp. confirmed the constitution of the tribunal in a statement issued late Wednesday but otherwise declined to comment on the substance of the proceeding. The Argentine Procuración del Tesoro did not respond to a request for comment.

### Sidebar: The Argentine Procuración del Tesoro's Mining-Sector Defense Posture

---

### Sidebar: The Argentine Procuración del Tesoro's Mining-Sector Defense Posture

The Argentine Procuración del Tesoro de la Nación — the office of the federal solicitor general — has, since approximately 2019, adopted a distinctively aggressive jurisdictional defense posture in mining-sector investment-treaty proceedings. Procurador del Tesoro **Dr. Hipólito Casarrubias-Mendizábal**, in office since the change of government in December 2023, has publicly stated that the denial-of-benefits jurisprudence developed by Argentine federal prosecutors in *Cariboo Northern Resources* and earlier matters represents what he called, in a January 2024 address at the Buenos Aires Bar Association, "the most important strategic asset in the defense of the Argentine fisc against speculative mining claims."

The denial-of-benefits jurisdictional theory — under which a respondent state argues that a claimant nominally incorporated in a BIT-protected jurisdiction is in substance owned or controlled by nationals of a non-protected third state — has been a feature of investment-treaty practice for more than two decades. Recent Argentine practice has, however, pressed the theory in directions that the broader investment-treaty bar regards as novel. In particular, the Procuración has, in the *Cariboo Northern* matter, advanced an argument that beneficial ownership for denial-of-benefits purposes should be assessed by reference to the underlying funding source rather than to the holder of the equity interest. This argument, which has not yet been adjudicated, would, if accepted, substantially expand the practical reach of the denial-of-benefits clause.

### Sidebar: Lithium Concession Cancellations Across the Andean Triangle

The cancellation of the Salar Norte Litio SA concessions in November 2022 was the first in a series of provincial-level mining concession cancellations across the Andean lithium triangle (Argentina, Bolivia, Chile) over the last eighteen months. The following table summarises publicly reported cancellations:

| Date | Country | Province / Region | Concession holder | Approximate area |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nov 2022 | Argentina | Salta | Salar Norte Litio SA (North Andes Lithium Corp.) | 47,000 ha |
| Feb 2023 | Bolivia | Potosí | Salar Boliviano SRL (FRESLi-Bolivia subsidiary) | 22,400 ha |
| May 2023 | Chile | Atacama | Litio del Desierto SpA (Albemarle subsidiary) | 18,900 ha |
| Aug 2023 | Argentina | Jujuy | Norandina Litio SA (Cariboo Northern subsidiary) | 31,200 ha |
| Oct 2023 | Argentina | Catamarca | Catamarca Lithium Resources SA (Toronto-listed) | 28,700 ha |

Each of these cancellations has either generated or is expected to generate investment-treaty arbitration; the Procuración del Tesoro estimates the cumulative claim exposure at approximately $9.8 billion across the five matters.

### Sidebar: The "Strategic Minerals" Provincial Legislation

A significant background development for the YMF / North Andes proceeding is the November 2021 passage by the Salta provincial legislature of Provincial Law No. 8624, the "Ley de Minerales Estratégicos," which designates lithium and several other minerals as "strategic" for provincial-development purposes and authorizes the provincial government to renegotiate or cancel existing mining concessions in order to "align them with provincial-development priorities."

The constitutionality of the Strategic Minerals Law has been the subject of substantial legal commentary, and at least three federal-court actions have been filed against the law in Argentine federal court. The Procuración del Tesoro takes the position that the Strategic Minerals Law represents a lawful exercise of provincial regulatory authority, consistent with both the Argentine constitutional architecture and with applicable international-law standards.

### Sidebar: Third-Party Funding Disclosure Issues

### Sidebar: Third-Party Funding Disclosure Issues

The substantial third-party funding presence in the YMF / North Andes matter — and in mining-sector investment-treaty proceedings generally — is expected to give rise to procedural skirmishing over the scope of disclosure obligations under the ICC's 2021 rules on funder identification. The ICC's 2021 rules require disclosure of the existence of third-party funding and of the identity of the funder, but the precise scope of the disclosure obligation — including in particular whether it extends to the funder's commercial terms, to the funder's beneficial owners, and to the existence of any conflicts between the funder and the arbitrators — remains the subject of considerable practical uncertainty.

A separate but related question concerns the use of third-party funding to finance respondent defense costs in investment-treaty proceedings. The Argentine Procuración del Tesoro has, to the author's knowledge, not disclosed any third-party funding of its defense costs in the YMF / North Andes matter or in any other pending investment-treaty proceeding.

### Sidebar: A Note on the Chair Selection Process

### Sidebar: A Note on the Chair Selection Process

ICC practice in chair selection — when the party-appointed co-arbitrators fail to agree — calls for the ICC Court to consult its internal databases and external consultations to identify a list of candidates with the relevant subject-matter expertise, linguistic capacity, and availability. The list of candidates publicly understood to have been considered for the YMF / North Andes chairmanship reflects the ICC Secretariat's typical practice of working with a candidate pool of five to eight senior arbitrators with substantial Latin American investment-treaty experience.

The final selection of Dr. Beilenberg-Roth is consistent with what practitioners describe as an ICC trend toward selecting continental European arbitrators with civil-law backgrounds for chair roles in Latin American treaty matters, on the theory that civil-law-trained arbitrators are better positioned to navigate the substantive and procedural issues that arise in proceedings governed by treaty obligations and customary international law.

### Sidebar: A Note on the Argentine BIT Network

### Sidebar: A Note on the Argentine BIT Network

The Argentina–Canada BIT, on which North Andes's claim is grounded, is one of approximately fifty bilateral investment treaties to which Argentina is a party. The treaty was signed on November 5, 1991, and entered into force on April 29, 1993. Article 12 of the treaty contains a denial-of-benefits clause that is broadly typical of the Canadian model BIT of the early 1990s.

The Argentine BIT network has historically generated more investment-treaty arbitration than the network of any other state. As of December 2022, Argentina had been a respondent in 64 publicly known ICSID proceedings and an additional 19 non-ICSID investment-treaty proceedings (under UNCITRAL, ICC, or SCC rules), the vast majority of which arose under bilateral investment treaties. The cumulative claim value of pending proceedings against Argentina is estimated, as noted above, at approximately $11.8 billion as of May 2023.

Argentine practice in the defense of investment-treaty claims has evolved substantially over the past two decades. Following the 2001–2002 economic crisis and the wave of claims that followed it, Argentina developed substantial in-house and contracted-counsel capacity to defend major investment-treaty proceedings. The current configuration — under which the Procuración del Tesoro retains overall responsibility for defense strategy and delegates particular matters to specialised external counsel — was established in approximately 2015.

### Sidebar: The Brazilian Energy Sector and Investment-Treaty Considerations

### Sidebar: The Brazilian Energy Sector and Investment-Treaty Considerations

Although the present matter concerns Argentina, it is worth noting that Brazil — the home jurisdiction of the respondent in the other ICC matter discussed above involving Professor Vance — is not a party to any ICSID Convention investment-treaty network. Brazil has, however, concluded a number of "Cooperation and Facilitation Investment Agreements" (CFIAs) over the past several years that include dispute-resolution mechanisms, though these mechanisms are state-to-state rather than investor-state.

The absence of investor-state arbitration in the Brazilian BIT network means that investment-treaty disputes involving Brazilian state-owned enterprises are typically resolved through commercial arbitration under the institutional rules of bodies such as the ICC, rather than through investment-treaty arbitration under the ICSID or UNCITRAL rules.

### Sidebar: Reflections on the Mining-Sector Arbitration Wave

### Sidebar: Reflections on the Mining-Sector Arbitration Wave

Practitioners on both the claimant and respondent sides told Law360 that the current wave of mining-sector investment-treaty arbitration is likely to continue for at least three to five years. The post-2020 expansion of provincial-level concession granting in Latin America, combined with the post-2022 political shifts in several of the major mining jurisdictions, has produced a substantial pipeline of claims that are now beginning to materialise as formal proceedings.

"What we are seeing is a generational shift in the political economy of mining in the Andes," one Buenos Aires-based mining-law specialist told Law360. "The contracts that were written in the 2010s were written in a market in which lithium was a niche industrial mineral. Lithium is now a geopolitically strategic resource, and the political economy of the contracts has caught up with that reality."

The result, the specialist continued, is a wave of disputes that will likely keep the ICC, ICSID, and UNCITRAL caseloads in Latin American investment treaty matters elevated for the foreseeable future, with significant implications for the broader investment-treaty bar and for the major institutional arbitration providers.

### Sidebar: A Note on Provincial-Level Treaty Implementation in Argentina

A recurring legal-technical question in Argentine investment-treaty proceedings concerns the relationship between federal-level treaty obligations and provincial-level regulatory actions. Argentina is a federal state, and the country's twenty-three provinces (together with the Autonomous City of Buenos Aires) retain substantial regulatory authority over natural resources within their respective territories. The Argentine federal constitution explicitly recognises provincial ownership of natural resources within provincial territory (Article 124), and provincial governments routinely exercise concession-granting, taxation, and regulatory authority in the mining and hydrocarbons sectors.

The interaction between this federal-provincial division of authority and Argentina's international-law obligations under its BIT network has been the subject of considerable arbitral and academic analysis. The settled position — repeatedly affirmed in ICSID arbitral practice — is that the Argentine Republic remains internationally responsible for provincial-level conduct that breaches the country's treaty obligations, regardless of the domestic constitutional allocation of authority between the federal and provincial levels.

The Argentine Procuración del Tesoro has, however, advanced in a number of recent matters the position that the practical reality of federal-provincial relations should inform the analysis of treaty-compatibility questions, particularly where the underlying claim concerns alleged regulatory conduct by a provincial government rather than by the federal government itself. This position has not been broadly adopted by arbitral tribunals, but it remains a feature of Argentine defense practice and is expected to be advanced in the YMF / North Andes proceeding.

### Sidebar: A Note on Chinese Investment in Argentine Mining

### Sidebar: A Note on Chinese Investment in Argentine Mining

The respondent's denial-of-benefits jurisdictional objection — grounded in the allegation that North Andes Lithium Corp. is in substance owned or controlled by Chinese principals through a Toronto holding-company structure — raises a question of broader interest concerning the role of Chinese investment in the Argentine mining sector.

Chinese state-owned and state-affiliated investors have, over the past decade, become significant participants in the Argentine lithium and copper sectors. The China-Argentina bilateral relationship in the mining sector is, however, governed by a different legal architecture than the Argentina-Canada relationship that grounds the present proceeding. There is no investment-treaty arbitration mechanism between China and Argentina; Chinese investors who wish to access investment-treaty arbitration in respect of Argentine investments must therefore structure their holdings through a third jurisdiction, typically Canada or a Western European state, that is a party to a BIT with Argentina.

This structuring practice — sometimes referred to as "treaty shopping," though the term is not always precisely apt — is widely recognised as a feature of contemporary investment-treaty practice. The denial-of-benefits clause, present in many modern BITs and BIT-equivalent instruments, is the principal jurisdictional mechanism through which respondent states may challenge the entitlement of structured claimants to treaty protection.

The application of the denial-of-benefits clause to structures involving Chinese underlying ownership has been a subject of particular arbitral attention in recent years, with several recent awards reaching nuanced — and not always consistent — conclusions about the scope of the clause's application. The YMF / North Andes tribunal will therefore have the opportunity to contribute to the developing body of arbitral law on this question, and practitioners told Law360 that they will be watching the tribunal's eventual analysis closely.

### Sidebar: A Brief Note on the Salar de Olaroz–Cauchari Basin

### Sidebar: A Brief Note on the Salar de Olaroz–Cauchari Basin

The disputed concessions cover approximately 47,000 hectares in the Salar de Olaroz–Cauchari basin, one of the principal lithium-brine deposits in the Andean lithium triangle. The basin straddles the border between Salta and Jujuy provinces in northern Argentina and is part of the broader Puna de Atacama plateau, which extends across portions of northern Argentina, southwestern Bolivia, and northern Chile.

The basin's lithium-bearing brines are extracted through a process of solar evaporation, in which brine is pumped from underground aquifers into a series of evaporation ponds and concentrated over a period of approximately twelve to eighteen months to produce lithium-rich concentrates suitable for downstream processing. The process is comparatively low-cost relative to hard-rock lithium extraction (the principal alternative production method, used primarily in Australia and in parts of Africa), and lithium produced from Andean brines accordingly enjoys a substantial cost advantage in the global lithium market.

The Salar de Olaroz–Cauchari basin has, since the late 2010s, been the subject of substantial international investment, principally from Australian, Canadian, Chinese, and Japanese-Korean operators. The basin currently hosts six operating or near-operating lithium-brine projects, with combined annual production capacity in the range of 90,000–110,000 tonnes lithium carbonate equivalent (LCE) as of mid-2023.

### Sidebar: Concluding Editorial Note

This Law360 report is based on conversations with three persons with direct knowledge of the constitution of the tribunal and on a review of publicly available documents concerning the underlying dispute, including the November 2022 Salta provincial decree cancelling the disputed concessions, the February 2023 request for arbitration filed by North Andes Lithium Corp., and the subsequent procedural orders issued by the tribunal through June 2023.
